text: Bilateral femoral arteries are marked prior to the operation and incisions made below the inguinal ligament.
It's important to expose the common femoral artery and not cannulate the profunda or the SFA.
When in doubt, be sure to follow the artery up towards the inguinal ligament to make sure it is the common femoral artery.
The femoral vein is exposed.
It's important to minimize extensive dissection.
This may cause lymphatic disruption.
Only the anterior surface of the femoral artery and the femoral vein need to be exposed.
A purse string suture is placed around the femoral artery and vein using a fluoroprolene suture.
For venous drainage, we use a 25 French venous cania for most patients.
I will routinely place the venous cannula on the surface of the patient to estimate the depth of insertion.
This gives me an idea of how deep to advance the cannula.
The femoral vein is then accessed via the Selinger technique and the wires advance up into the SVC.
Your echocardiographer should have a bicable view with good visualization of both the IVC and the SVC.
Once the venous cannula is advanced up the SVC under direct echo visualization, the cannula is then secured.
The femoral artery is then accessed via the Selinger technique.
The temptation will be to put the largest arterial cannula, but the goal should be to put the smallest cannula that will provide adequate perfusion.
As previously discussed, the ideal location should be roughly two and a half fingerbreadths below the right clavicle as seen here.
The right pleural space is carefully entered and the internal thoracic artery and vein subsequently ligated.
The rib is divided at the cost of cartilage using heavy scissors.
A soft tissue retractor is then placed followed by a rib retractor.
At times, there may be a thick layer of fat surrounding the pericardium.
It's important to remove this fat to facilitate adequate visualization.
The pericardium is then entered.
I found that the safest location to enter the pericardium is at the junction between the aorta and the right atrium to avoid injury to underlying structures.
It's important to visualize the phrenic nerve during this mobilization.
Pericardial retraction sutures are then placed.
It's important to be liberal with the placement of pericardial retraction sutures to pull the aorta into view.
As you can see here, with appropriate retraction sutures, you'll get excellent exposure of the aorta.
The next step is to dissect the plane for the aorta cross clamp.
The ideal location is above the right pulmonary artery to maximize the length of the aorta.
I use a bovie cautery, but mainly blunt metabalm scissors to find the plane for the cross clamp.
I then place a CO2 line in early to flood the operative field.
We simply attach a CO2 tubing to a 14 French angiocatheter and advance it through the chest wall as seen here.
A 4-0 proline perstring suture is then placed at the right superior pulmonary vein to vent the left ventricle.
And the LV vent is then carefully placed.
The cross clamp is then placed.
I then remove the antegrade cardioplegia needle and make my aortotomy at the same location as the needle site.
That way, when I close the aortotomy, the antegrade needle site is incorporated and subsequently hemostatic.
De-airing is performed via the LV vent in the right superior pulmonary vein.
The ideal location of the aortotomy is at the level of the aortic fat pad.
Aortic retraction sutures are then placed using foraproline sutures and secured to the chest wall.
and then place an aortic cuff to expose aortic leaflets.
As you can see here, there's excellent exposure to the aortic valve with this approach.
As you can see, this is a bicuspid aortic valve.
The aortic valve is then removed in the standard fashion, being careful to suction any remaining debris that might sit inside the left ventricle.
I will now discuss the aortic valve replacement using sutured biprosthetic valve and then discuss using a sutureless valve.
For the sutured aortic valve, sizing is performed in the standard fashion.
This patient sized for a 23 millimeter valve.
Pledgeated sutures are then placed at the respective commissures as seen here.
And then placed circumferentially around the aortic annulus.
Sutures are then placed through the aortic cuff and parachuted down to the annulus.
I like to remove the handle of the valve early.
This allows me to more easily see-saw the valve into the aortic annulus.
As usual, be sure to visualize both the left and right ostia.
The valve is then secured in the standard fashion with either core knot or a knot pusher.
If these conditions are met, then this is your valve size.
The next step involves balloon inflation for 30 seconds at 4 atmospheres for optimal sealing.
Visual inspection reveals a nicely positioned valve.
It's important to take your time when closing the aortotomy to obtain a perfect hemostatic closure.
Once your cross clamp is off and the heart is no longer decompressed, visualization of the aortotomy can be difficult, particularly near the aortal PA junction.
The canines are removed and incisions close in the standard fashion.
